edges.description: Reduced canonical WNT signaling fails to sustain the epithelial-mesenchymal signaling required to advance the tooth germ through initiation and bud stages, arresting odontogenesis.
Loss of EDA-EDAR-NF-kappaB signaling deprives the developing tooth germ of a required ectodermal-appendage signal, contributing to odontogenic arrest.
Loss of PAX9/MSX1 transcription-factor activity arrests the tooth germ at the bud stage and disrupts patterning, preventing progression of odontogenesis.
Arrest of tooth-germ development at the affected stage results in the congenital absence of the corresponding teeth.
When one to five tooth germs fail to develop, the clinical result is hypodontia.
When six or more tooth germs fail to develop, the clinical result is oligodontia.
Complete failure of tooth-germ development across the dentition produces anodontia, the most severe point on the tooth-agenesis spectrum.
Genotype-specific patterning defects arrest development of particular tooth classes, producing selective tooth agenesis.

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in AXIN2 cause familial tooth agenesis and predispose to colorectal cancer.
evidence.snippet: We show that oligodontia and predisposition to cancer are caused by a nonsense mutation, Arg656Stop, in the Wnt-signaling regulator AXIN2.

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in AXIN2 cause familial tooth agenesis and predispose to colorectal cancer.
evidence.snippet: Our findings introduce a new gene for hereditary colorectal cancer and suggest that tooth agenesis may be an indicator of cancer susceptibility.

nodes.description: Complete congenital absence of all teeth; the most severe and rarest form of tooth agenesis.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic analysis: Wnt and other pathways in nonsyndromic tooth agenesis.
evidence.snippet: Tooth agenesis (TA) is one of the most common developmental anomalies that affects the number of teeth.

nodes.description: The transcription factors PAX9 (paired-box) and MSX1 (homeodomain) act in the dental mesenchyme to drive the bud-to-cap transition and integrate BMP/WNT signaling. Heterozygous loss-of-function variants arrest tooth development at the bud stage and disrupt patterning, producing selective agenesis with characteristic class-specific (e.g., molar, premolar) patterns.

evidence.reference_title: A human MSX1 homeodomain missense mutation causes selective tooth agenesis.
evidence.snippet: We propose that the Arg31 Pro mutatrion comprises MSX1 interactions, and suggest that MSX1 functions are critical for normal development of specific human teeth.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical, radiographic, and genetic evaluation of a novel form of autosomal-dominant oligodontia.
evidence.snippet: A frameshift mutation recently identified within the paired domain of the transcription factor, PAX9, has been linked to a unique form of oligodontia

nodes.description: The ectodysplasin pathway transduces EDA ligand binding to EDAR through the EDARADD adaptor to activate NF-kappaB, a signal required for ectodermal- appendage and tooth-germ development. X-linked EDA loss of function abrogates this signal and impairs odontogenesis, ranging from selective tooth agenesis to the dental component of hypohidrotic ectodermal dysplasia.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic analysis: Wnt and other pathways in nonsyndromic tooth agenesis.
evidence.snippet: An extensive analysis of publicly accessible databases revealed 15 causative genes responsible for nonsyndromic TA, along with their signaling pathways in Wnt/β-catenin, TGF-β/BMP, and Eda/Edar/NF-κB.

nodes.description: Mild form: congenital absence of one to five teeth (excluding third molars). This is the most common presentation of tooth agenesis.

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in WNT10A are present in more than half of isolated hypodontia cases.
evidence.snippet: Dental agenesis is the most common, often heritable, developmental anomaly in humans.

nodes.description: Convergent consequence of WNT, EDA-NF-kappaB, and mesenchymal transcription- factor deficiencies: the reciprocal epithelial-mesenchymal signaling that drives the dental lamina through the initiation, bud, cap, and bell stages fails, arresting tooth-germ development. Because tooth number is fixed during embryogenesis, the resulting absence of teeth is non-progressive.

evidence.reference_title: Tooth agenesis: An overview of diagnosis, aetiology and management.
evidence.snippet: Patients with one or more developmentally absent teeth are routinely encountered in dental practice.

evidence.reference_title: A human MSX1 homeodomain missense mutation causes selective tooth agenesis.
evidence.snippet: We demonstrate that a mutation in the homeobox gene, MSX1, causes a common developmental anomaly, familial tooth agenesis.

evidence.reference_title: A human MSX1 homeodomain missense mutation causes selective tooth agenesis.
evidence.snippet: Sequence analyses demonstrated an Arg31Pro missense mutation in the homeodomain of MSX1 in all affected family members.

nodes.description: Severe form: congenital absence of six or more teeth (excluding third molars). Often associated with WNT10A, PAX9, MSX1, LRP6, and AXIN2 variants.

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in AXIN2 cause familial tooth agenesis and predispose to colorectal cancer.
evidence.snippet: severe permanent tooth agenesis (oligodontia) and colorectal neoplasia segregate with dominant inheritance

nodes.description: Agenesis restricted to particular tooth classes (commonly second premolars, maxillary lateral incisors, and molars), reflecting genotype-specific patterning defects.

evidence.reference_title: A human MSX1 homeodomain missense mutation causes selective tooth agenesis.
evidence.snippet: Genetic linkage analyses in a family with autosomal dominant agenesis of second premolars and third molars identified a locus on chromosome 4p

nodes.description: Congenital absence of one or more teeth, the defining manifestation, detected when expected teeth fail to develop and erupt.

evidence.reference_title: Tooth agenesis: An overview of diagnosis, aetiology and management.
evidence.snippet: Patients with one or more developmentally absent teeth are routinely encountered in dental practice.

nodes.description: Canonical WNT/beta-catenin signaling is central to the reciprocal epithelial- mesenchymal interactions that initiate and pattern the tooth germ. Loss-of- function variants in the WNT ligands WNT10A and WNT10B and the co-receptor LRP6 reduce pathway activation, while truncating AXIN2 variants dysregulate the pathway; either inhibition or aberrant stimulation of WNT signaling can prevent normal tooth development.

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in AXIN2 cause familial tooth agenesis and predispose to colorectal cancer.
evidence.snippet: The results provide the first evidence of the importance of Wnt signaling for the development of dentition in humans

evidence.reference_title: Concurrent manifestation of oligodontia and thrombocytopenia caused by a contiguous gene deletion in 12p13.2: A three-generation clinical report.
evidence.snippet: Wnt and Wnt-associated pathways play an important role in the genetic etiology of oligodontia, a severe form of tooth agenesis.

nodes.description: De novo frameshift insertion in AXIN2 identified in an unrelated young patient with severe tooth agenesis.
Missense mutation in the MSX1 homeodomain at a highly conserved residue that contacts target DNA; segregates with familial tooth agenesis of second premolars and third molars.
Nonsense mutation in AXIN2 segregating with oligodontia and colorectal neoplasia in a Finnish family; expected to activate WNT signaling.
